title: Other Services Related to Advertising

alternatives.name: Promotional-products and advertising-specialties distributors
inclusion_criteria: The firm primarily develops promotional-product ideas, buys branded or brandable products from outside suppliers, coordinates decoration or customization, and resells finished items to end buyers.

Advertising-specialties distribution or coordination of the production and delivery of premiums is the firm's principal business, rather than incidental merchandise attached to an advertising agency, printer, manufacturer, or fulfillment company.
The firm falls within the normalized dataset's 20-499 employee proxy for the $1-10M EBITDA band.
exclusion_criteria: Exclude firms primarily manufacturing, printing, importing for wholesale, or decorating products for sale through independent distributors when distribution to end buyers is not their principal activity.

Exclude advertising agencies, direct-mail providers, advertising-material delivery firms, display-advertising operators, marketing consultants, and media buyers or representatives classified elsewhere.
Exclude sign-lettering shops, miscellaneous advertising-service firms, welcoming services, firms outside the target-band proxy, and firms whose principal activity cannot be verified.
alternatives.rationale: The normalized dataset provides a LOW-quality target-band anchor of 482 firms. The 2022 Economic Census form still identifies advertising-specialties goods distributors as a distinct 541890 primary activity, and the current NAPCS product boundary describes coordination of production and delivery of promotional premiums. The last detailed national subtype table found, the 2002 Economic Census, reported 4,419 advertising-specialties establishments out of 8,500 in 541890. Applying that 52.0% share to 482 gives 250.6, rounded to 251. The 170-330 range is a bounded judgment widened for the two-decade vintage, establishment-to-firm conversion, target-band size skew, and classification changes; the trade-association definition supports the current operating boundary but not the target-band count.

alternatives.name: Sign, display, and storefront lettering service shops
inclusion_criteria: The firm primarily applies hand-rendered or computer-generated graphics or lettering to signs, storefronts, vehicles, offices, or trade-show displays for advertising purposes.
Service revenue from custom lettering, painting, or graphics application predominates over the manufacture of signs or the sale of advertising media space.
The firm falls within the normalized dataset's 20-499 employee proxy for the $1-10M EBITDA band.
exclusion_criteria: Exclude electric and nonelectric sign manufacturers, commercial printers, graphic-design studios, display-advertising media owners, and building-installation contractors when those are the principal activities.
Exclude promotional-products distributors, miscellaneous advertising-service firms, welcoming services, firms outside the target-band proxy, and unresolved classifications.
Exclude businesses for which lettering is incidental to vehicle repair, painting, construction, retail merchandise, or another principal activity.
alternatives.rationale: The 2022 Economic Census form retains sign painting and lettering as a specific 541890 primary activity, and NAPCS defines display lettering as applying graphics or lettering to signage by hand or computer-generated means. The 2002 Economic Census reported 2,844 sign-painting and lettering establishments out of 8,500 in 541890. Applying that 33.5% share to the 482-firm band anchor gives 161.3, rounded to 161. The 100-250 range reflects the same vintage, unit, size-band, and reclassification limitations and overlaps the selected archetype's range.

alternatives.name: Merchandise-demonstration, visual-merchandising, and other residual advertising-service firms
inclusion_criteria: The firm primarily provides merchandise demonstrations, store-window dressing, mannequin decorating, visual-merchandising labor, or another advertising-related service not classified to a more specific advertising industry.
The firm's principal activity is a fee-for-service advertising support function and not promotional-products distribution, sign lettering, or welcoming services.
The firm falls within the normalized dataset's 20-499 employee proxy for the $1-10M EBITDA band.
exclusion_criteria: Exclude advertising agencies, public-relations agencies, media services, display advertising, direct mail, advertising-material delivery, and marketing consulting classified elsewhere.
Exclude promotional-products distributors, sign-lettering shops, welcoming services, firms outside the target-band proxy, and unresolved classifications.
Exclude staffing, event-production, retail-fixture, or logistics firms when advertising support is not their principal activity.
alternatives.rationale: NAPCS separately identifies merchandise demonstrations and other advertising services not elsewhere classified. The 2002 Economic Census reported 1,143 all-other advertising-service establishments out of 8,500 in 541890. Applying that 13.4% share to 482 gives 64.8, rounded to 65. The 20-130 range is wide because the catch-all boundary can absorb new services and classification changes, and the historical table is not target-band firm evidence.

residual.name: Welcoming services and unresolved or mixed 541890 firms
residual.rationale: The 2002 Economic Census reported 94 welcoming-service establishments out of 8,500, implying 5.3 of the 482-firm band anchor and rounded to five. The residual also retains firms that cannot be assigned by principal activity or mix otherwise excluded functions. Its broad range reflects the obsolete subtype table and uncertainty around mixed-service classifications.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form PS-54180: Advertising and Related Services
2002 Economic Census: Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services, United States
NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54189: Other Services Related to Advertising
Promotional Products Association International Distributor Definition
PPAI Branded Merchandise by the Numbers

target_archetype.selection_basis: The normalized dataset estimates 482 target-band firms with LOW quality. Three mutually exclusive archetypes follow the 2022 Census primary-activity choices and NAPCS service boundaries; a named residual retains welcoming and unresolved firms. Base counts apply the 2002 national establishment shares to 482: advertising specialties 482 x (4,419 / 8,500) = 250.6; sign lettering 482 x (2,844 / 8,500) = 161.3; all other advertising services 482 x (1,143 / 8,500) = 64.8; and welcoming services 482 x (94 / 8,500) = 5.3. Rounded bases reconcile to 482. Advertising-specialties distributors have the largest base and are selected. Their 170-330 range overlaps the sign-lettering range of 100-250, so selection_uncertainty is true. No whole-NAICS economic claim is authorized.

value_basis.scope: Employee and controllable-contractor cash cost used to deliver the selected advertising-specialties distributor's controlled value added, with employee and contractor pools kept distinct and every role assigned by primary function.
value_basis.controllable_value_added_boundary: Controllable value-added revenue equals recognized revenue less only documented third-party amounts for which the distributor acts economically as an agent and does not control inventory, pricing, production specifications, provider selection, quality, or delivery risk. Ordinary promotional merchandise, printing, decoration, production, kitting, fulfillment, and freight costs remain inside the boundary when the distributor selects or directs providers, takes title or inventory risk, controls specifications or quality, bundles the charge, or earns a product or service margin. Any amount deducted from revenue is excluded from both cash-cost IDs, and gross billings may not silently replace a missing or unbounded denominator.
approved_pass_through_categories: Third-party media, sponsorship, or placement spend paid under documented agency terms with no inventory, pricing, performance, or credit risk retained by the distributor

Customer-directed outside merchandise, printing, decoration, or production spend that is separately identified, reimbursed without service margin, and not selected, specified, controlled, warranted, or risk-borne by the distributor
Itemized third-party postage, carrier freight, customs duty, or brokerage reimbursed without markup where the distributor bears no routing, loss, delay, or delivery-performance risk

Customer-directed third-party storage, kitting, or fulfillment reimbursed without markup where the distributor neither selects nor manages the provider and bears no inventory, quality, or service-level risk

roles.title: Sales, account development, and client strategy
roles.rationale: Primary function is prospecting, account planning, promotional-product ideation with clients, quoting, negotiation, closing, and relationship ownership.

roles.title: Order management, project coordination, and client service
roles.rationale: Primary function is order entry, timeline ownership, proof and approval coordination, status communication, exception handling, and post-order service.

roles.title: Product sourcing, procurement, and supplier management
roles.rationale: Primary function is product search, supplier selection, purchase-order control, availability and price confirmation, vendor compliance, and supplier issue resolution.

roles.title: Creative, artwork, proofing, and production preparation
roles.rationale: Primary function is concept presentation, artwork preparation, proof review, specification control, and production-ready file coordination.

roles.title: Controlled fulfillment, warehouse, logistics, and quality support
roles.rationale: Primary function is distributor-controlled receiving, inspection, kitting, inventory, shipment coordination, returns, and quality administration; amounts validly deducted as pass-through are outside this role's cash-cost pool.

roles.title: General management, finance, people, systems, legal, and administration
roles.rationale: Primary function is enterprise leadership and shared support not attributable to a client, order, creative, sourcing, or controlled-fulfillment role.

value_basis.rationale: No target-band employee and contractor cash-cost ledger by function was available before economic research, and the dataset's occupation mix is only at the broader four-digit 5418 level. The six-role taxonomy and weights are therefore authoritative class-neutral assumptions with ESTIMATE method, NONE quality, and no sources. The weights sum to 1.00 across the combined employee and controllable-contractor pools; each employee or contractor is assigned once by primary function. Target records are required to replace the assumption.
caveats: Assumption mode, ESTIMATE method, NONE quality, empty source IDs, and empty value-basis sources must be preserved unless target-specific evidence supports a formally authorized replacement.
An approved category is only an eligibility boundary, not evidence of an amount. Each deduction still requires target contracts, accounting records, and a bounded principal-versus-agent analysis.

Ordinary merchandise, media, production, decoration, logistics, or fulfillment cost is not pass-through merely because an outside provider performs it; distributor control, risk, bundling, or margin keeps it inside controllable value added.
No amount may appear both as a deduction in controllable value-added revenue and as controllable-contractor cost in the cash-cost numerator.
Owner labor is included when paid through payroll or a service contract; owner distributions, interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization are outside cash cost.
Selection ranges overlap, so any eventual target-specific replacement must also bound the effect of plausible reclassification to the sign-lettering alternative or remain MISSING.

source_hints.source: Contemporary Census primary-activity tabulations, business-register microdata, or a reproducible census of 20-499 employee 541890 firms split among codes 541890 201, 541890 301, and 541890 801
source_hints.why: The base allocation bridges a 2002 establishment subtype mix to a LOW-quality 2022 firm-band estimate; contemporary size-specific firm counts are needed to resolve selection.

source_hints.source: Target-company audited or reviewed financial statements, revenue-recognition policies, general-ledger detail, supplier invoices, purchase orders, customer contracts, freight and duty records, rebates, and principal-versus-agent analyses
source_hints.why: A promotional-products distributor may report customer billings gross while substantial consideration is owed to outside product, decoration, freight, and fulfillment providers; only documented, noncontrolled amounts may be deducted to derive controllable value-added revenue.

source_hints.source: Target-firm payroll registers, benefit and payroll-tax schedules, commission plans, owner-compensation normalization, contractor ledgers, and function-tagged staffing rosters
source_hints.why: These records are necessary to replace the frozen role-weight assumption and to separate controlled contractor work from denominator pass-through without double counting.

source_hints.source: Order-to-cash process maps, product-search logs, quote histories, proof cycles, purchase-order records, vendor acknowledgments, shipment exceptions, return records, service levels, and system inventories
source_hints.why: Distributor workflows span clients and outside suppliers; implementation feasibility depends on handoffs, approval rights, data quality, exceptions, and supplier interfaces.

source_hints.source: Customer master agreements, bid and renewal histories, pricing files, gross-profit reports, supplier-price changes, rebates, client-concentration schedules, and win-loss records
source_hints.why: Client repricing, competitive rebids, supplier changes, and contractual pass-through determine the commercially retained share of any operating benefit.

source_hints.source: Closed-deal databases, broker materials, ownership records, franchise and affiliate agreements, add-on histories, and buyer outreach logs for promotional-products distributors
source_hints.why: The industry includes very small, affiliated, and lifestyle businesses; sale availability must be separated from a reference buyer's practical access and win rate in the target band.

source_hints.source: Purchase agreements, quality-of-earnings reports, lender cases, customer and supplier retention cohorts, salesperson book-transfer data, assignment terms, and post-transaction operating records
source_hints.why: Paid-price robustness and year-five survival depend on customer relationships, supplier access, salesperson portability, and constant-price demand that are not established by industry counts.

controllable_value_added: What revenue is recognized under the target firm's accounting policy, and does the firm act as principal or agent for merchandise, decoration, freight, duties, fulfillment, rebates, and other supplier charges?

For each proposed pass-through category, what contract, invoice, ledger, and control evidence supports base, low, and high amounts owed to an outside provider and not controlled as operator value added?

After excluding documented pass-through without subtracting any cash cost twice, is controllable value-added revenue positive, bounded, and internally reconciled to recognized revenue in every scenario?
What employee compensation and associated removable cash burden is attributable to the selected archetype on the same accounting period and entity boundary as controllable value-added revenue?
Which contractor costs deliver operator-controlled value added rather than supplier product, decoration, freight, duty, or fulfillment pass-through, and how are ambiguous vendors classified?

What target-specific evidence supports mutually exclusive role weights that sum to one, and which inputs must remain ASSUMPTION or MISSING if payroll, contractor, or functional records are unavailable?
operational_realization: Which product discovery, quoting, artwork, proof, purchase-order, vendor-confirmation, shipment, billing, collections, and returns workflows are performed by the selected archetype?
What client approvals, supplier interfaces, data rights, service levels, system dependencies, exception rates, and quality controls constrain operating changes?
What staffing, training, transition sequence, parallel operation, and recurring oversight would be required without interrupting customer orders or supplier commitments?
commercial_retention: How quickly do customer contracts, bids, and renewals reprice after changes in distributor cost, supplier pricing, service levels, or competitive offers?
What share of operating benefit is contractually passed through, competitively conceded, retained in gross profit, or offset by added service obligations in each of five years?
How do customer concentration, salesperson ownership of accounts, supplier rebates, order mix, and renewal cohorts support the low, base, and high retention schedules?
sale_availability: How many independently owned promotional-products distributors in the target band were available for sale in recent comparable periods, and how many were proprietary, broadly marketed, or add-on transactions?
Which ownership, affiliation, succession, customer-concentration, supplier-access, and minimum-scale conditions distinguish genuinely sale-available firms from the enumerated archetype?
What closed-deal and marketed-process evidence supports the low, base, and high sale-availability shares without treating all estimated firms as for sale?
buyer_access_win: What geography, ownership, affiliation, customer-concentration, supplier-access, and minimum-scale filters define a reference buyer's accessible target pool?
What outreach, response, process-participation, bid, financing, and closing evidence supports the buyer's low, base, and high access-and-win shares?
How are sale availability and buyer access-and-win measured separately so that the same accessibility limitation is not counted twice?
valuation_robustness: What paid multiples and transaction structures were observed for target-band promotional-products distributors after normalizing owner compensation, working capital, earn-outs, and acquired debt?

How should downside exit value reflect state-consistent changes in customer retention, supplier terms, salesperson continuity, pass-through accounting, and gross-profit mix without assuming multiple expansion?
What explicit population, period, unit, geography, and business-model bridge supports any adjacent transaction evidence?
operator_survival: Which customer relationships and supplier privileges are institutional versus dependent on an owner, salesperson, buying group, franchise, or individual vendor contact?
What assignment, change-of-control, termination, non-solicitation, rebate, credit, exclusivity, and minimum-volume terms govern continuity after ownership or operating-model change?

What evidence supports constant-price year-five demand for operator-controlled ideation, sourcing, coordination, creative, service, and fulfillment functions after removing merchandise and other documented pass-through volume?

special_notes: The primary record describes only target-band promotional-products and advertising-specialties distributors, not NAICS 541890 as a whole.

The largest-base rule selects advertising-specialties distributors at 251 firms over sign-lettering shops at 161 and other advertising-service firms at 65. Overlapping 170-330 and 100-250 ranges make selection uncertain.

The count bridge applies a 2002 establishment subtype mix to the normalized dataset's LOW-quality 2022 firm-band estimate. The ranges are bounded judgments, not sampling confidence intervals, and no contemporary target-band subtype census was found.

Recognized revenue, pass-through amounts, and controllable value-added revenue are not frozen as facts in this specification. Approved categories define eligibility only; each amount requires target-specific support, an input with no defensible bound is MISSING, and gross customer billings may not silently replace controllable value-added revenue.

Outside merchandise, media, production, decoration, freight, duties, storage, kitting, and fulfillment are not automatically pass-through. Deduction requires documented agency economics with no distributor control, risk, bundling, or service margin, and a deducted amount cannot also enter employee or controllable-contractor cash cost.

The value basis is an authoritative class-neutral assumption with distinct employee and controllable-contractor cost IDs, six mutually exclusive primary-function roles whose weights sum to 1.00, ESTIMATE method, NONE quality, and no sources. The broader four-digit 5418 occupation mix is not a valid deterministic substitute.

PPAI industry totals and definitions support current business-structure context only. They are broader than NAICS 541890, include suppliers and firms outside the target band, and do not determine the archetype count.
